title: On the Southward Flight of Dragons, and the Affliction Commonly Styled Ashscale
author: Maester Baelor Targaryen
summary: A scholarly examination of the recent, unexplained southward migration of dragons, and the mysterious dulling condition of their hides provisionally named Ashscale.
content:
Among the many certainties that scholars have long held concerning dragons, few appeared more reliable than the belief that these creatures, though capable of traversing immense distances, seldom abandoned territories favorable to their nature without compelling reason. Their tempers, breeding grounds, and preferred climates have varied throughout the centuries, yet the broad patterns of their movements have remained remarkably constant within the limits of recorded history.

It is therefore with considerable hesitation that I commit the following observations to parchment, for they concern a phenomenon that neither I nor any living scholar can adequately explain.

For nearly fifteen years, dragons throughout the known world have demonstrated an increasing inclination to depart from their customary ranges and fly southward. I write southward with deliberate care, for many readers will naturally assume I refer only to Dorne. Such an assumption would be mistaken. The journeys in question extend far beyond the deserts of Dorne, beyond the Stepstones, beyond even the shores of Lys, into waters and coastlines so distant that few Westerosi vessels venture there with any regularity.

Precisely how far these dragons travel remains uncertain. No maester has accompanied such a journey, nor has any account yet returned from the southern seas with sufficient detail to permit certainty. Nevertheless, reports have accumulated in such number that they deserve neither dismissal nor blind acceptance. Merchant captains, free traders, pearl divers, and sailors arriving from distant ports have independently described enormous winged creatures descending upon isolated coastlines before disappearing once more toward warmer horizons. The descriptions vary in detail, as all eyewitness accounts inevitably do, yet their central observation remains remarkably consistent: dragons are flying farther south than they once did.

Whether every reported beast was indeed a dragon cannot be known. The prudent scholar must resist the temptation to treat every sailor's tale as established fact. Men at sea are susceptible to fear, exhaustion, poor visibility, and the embellishments that accompany stories retold in crowded taverns. Yet skepticism must not become obstinacy. When independent witnesses, separated by oceans, kingdoms, and language, begin describing the same unusual occurrence, the matter demands investigation rather than ridicule.

During my years at the Citadel, I devoted no small portion of my studies to the examination of earlier records in search of similar migrations. The archives preserve thousands of letters, ship manifests, personal journals, raven correspondence, and travelogues spanning centuries. Most proved irrelevant. Some contained descriptions so fantastical that they scarcely warranted consideration. A handful, however, merit discussion.

Several fragmented accounts speak of isolated dragons appearing unexpectedly in distant southern waters during earlier centuries. Yet these incidents differ fundamentally from what we presently observe. Each concerns an individual beast whose movements may be explained by injury, unusual temperament, or simple chance. Never have I found convincing evidence that numerous dragons altered their habits within the same generation.

If such a migration has occurred before, it has escaped the surviving record. It is this absence that lends the present phenomenon its greatest significance.

As reports multiplied over recent years, another pattern slowly emerged. A surprising number of witnesses described dragons whose scales appeared unusually dull. Their hides, ordinarily possessing a sheen comparable to polished stone or tempered steel, instead reflected light unevenly, as though coated in fine ash. Others reported patches where the scales appeared pale, brittle, or roughened, particularly around the neck, shoulders, and the joints of the wings. Whether these observations reflect genuine physical changes or merely the distortions of great distance remains uncertain, yet the recurrence of such descriptions cannot easily be ignored.

It was for this reason that several of my learned colleagues adopted the provisional designation Ashscale when referring to the supposed condition. The name ought not be misunderstood. It was never intended to imply that the disease has been isolated, understood, or even conclusively demonstrated. Rather, it serves only as a convenient label under which observations may be gathered and discussed. Scholars require names not because names confer certainty, but because orderly discourse demands them. To speak of "the unexplained alteration affecting certain dragons observed during their southern migrations" in every correspondence would burden both writer and reader alike.

Thus, Ashscale entered scholarly usage. Whether it denotes a single disease, several unrelated ailments sharing similar outward signs, or perhaps no disease at all, remains wholly unknown.

It must also be acknowledged that not every dragon participating in these southern flights has been reported to display visible signs of deterioration. Some witnesses describe magnificent beasts whose scales gleamed no differently than those recorded in older chronicles. Others insist that every dragon they observed appeared weathered and diminished. Such contradictions are neither surprising nor fatal to the inquiry. They merely remind us that observations made at great distance, often under difficult conditions, seldom achieve perfect agreement.

The greatest danger confronting any scholar investigating this matter is not ignorance, but certainty acquired too quickly. Our understanding of dragons remains fragmentary despite centuries of study. We possess measurements of their bones, descriptions of their anatomy, and chronicles of their behavior in war, yet these amount only to scattered pieces of a far larger puzzle. A creature capable of flight despite its immense mass, whose breath kindles living flame, has never yielded its deepest mysteries willingly. It would therefore be an act of arrogance to imagine that one unusual migration could be explained after only fifteen years of observation.

Nevertheless, inquiry must begin somewhere. The pages that follow examine the principal explanations presently debated amongst the learned men of Westeros. Some are supported by careful observation; others rest upon inference alone. None, in my judgment, has yet earned the distinction of certainty.

I have written at considerable length elsewhere concerning the reports collected during these fifteen years, the testimonies of sailors and merchants, the anatomical peculiarities of dragons, and the reliability of the evidence upon which this inquiry rests. To repeat those arguments in their entirety would serve neither the reader nor the purpose of the present treatise. It shall suffice to summarize those conclusions most relevant to the principal question: what compels dragons to undertake these extraordinary southern journeys?

Among the explanations presently debated within learned circles, two have endured longer than the others. Neither has escaped criticism, yet both continue to account for a greater number of observations than any competing proposal presently known to me.

The first, and perhaps the most widely accepted among my colleagues, concerns the physiology of the dragon's scales. It has long been observed that healthy dragons possess scales of remarkable resilience and luster. Such qualities are unlikely to arise from horn alone. I have therefore argued, together with several esteemed colleagues, that dragons produce beneath their scales a natural oily secretion whose purpose is to preserve flexibility, resist cracking, and protect the living tissues between the scales from excessive drying.

Should this assumption prove correct, Ashscale may interfere with that process. The visible dulling of the scales reported by numerous witnesses, together with descriptions of roughened patches and apparent discomfort, may indicate not the destruction of the scales themselves but the gradual failure of those unseen tissues responsible for maintaining them. The scales endure; the living structures beneath them suffer.

Under this hypothesis, the dragons' remarkable journeys are not undertaken in pursuit of warmth alone. Rather, they seek seas whose exceptional warmth and mineral composition temporarily soothe the irritation caused by the affliction. Such waters would offer relief without constituting a cure. One need not suppose that dragons understand the nature of their illness any more than wounded men understand the healing properties of certain herbs. Instinct alone may guide them toward conditions under which discomfort is lessened.

The second explanation proceeds from an altogether different foundation. Several respected natural philosophers have proposed that Ashscale is not principally a disorder of the dragon itself, but the consequence of some minute parasite inhabiting the tissues beneath the scales. Whether such organisms are visible only through means presently unknown, or whether they escape ordinary examination by reason of their extraordinary smallness, cannot yet be answered.

If this theory is true, then the southern migrations require little further explanation. The exceedingly warm and saline waters lying far beyond the familiar shipping routes of the Narrow Sea may prove hostile to these unseen organisms. Dragons afflicted by them would therefore experience gradual relief after prolonged immersion, not because the waters strengthen the dragon, but because they weaken whatever burden it carries.

I confess that this hypothesis possesses a certain elegance. It accounts for the apparent recurrence of the migrations without requiring permanent injury to the dragons themselves. Yet elegance alone cannot establish truth. Despite repeated examinations of scales recovered after natural shedding, no scholar has yet produced convincing evidence of such a creature's existence. Until such evidence is obtained, the theory must remain precisely what it presently is—a plausible conjecture.

It deserves mention that neither of these explanations regards Ashscale as a condition ordinarily fatal to dragons. Indeed, were dragons dying in significant numbers, no scholar would be required to demonstrate the fact. The skies themselves would bear witness. Throughout these fifteen years, dragons have continued to fly, hunt, breed, and, above all, survive. What distinguishes the affliction is not its lethality, but the profound physiological burden it appears to impose upon otherwise formidable creatures.

Accounts gathered from dragonkeepers, shepherds, and seasoned mariners consistently describe afflicted dragons as unusually restless. Some remained grounded for longer periods than expected. Others appeared reluctant to fly except when beginning their long southern passage. A few were said to rub their flanks against stone outcroppings or descend repeatedly into warm coastal waters before resuming flight. Such reports, while individually imperfect, display a consistency difficult to ignore.

There exist, it is true, scattered tales claiming that one or two dragons never returned from the southern seas. Whether these beasts succumbed to their affliction, perished through age or misfortune, or simply established themselves in lands beyond the knowledge of Westerosi chroniclers, no honest man can determine. I have found no account sufficiently reliable to conclude that Ashscale itself ordinarily brings about death. It is for this reason that I urge restraint whenever certainty is proclaimed too readily.

The temptation to explain every unusual observation through a single elegant theory is among the oldest failings of scholarship. Nature seldom arranges herself according to the preferences of learned men.

Reports increasingly suggest that many dragons have begun returning to the regions from which they departed years ago. Whether this signifies that the affliction has passed, that it has merely entered another stage, or that our understanding of the entire phenomenon has been mistaken from the beginning, I cannot say. The evidence remains incomplete. Some may regard the return of the dragons as proof that the matter has ended.

The migration of dragons gave rise not only to scholarly inquiry, but also to speculation among those unaccustomed to distinguishing evidence from conviction. Among the common folk, the prevailing belief was that the dragons' departure could not be natural. In villages throughout the realm, many declared it a punishment sent by the gods. Some claimed that mankind had grown arrogant, presuming to command creatures that were never intended to bear riders. Others believed that the sins of kings had finally invited divine judgment, and that the dragons—being creatures of a higher design—had withdrawn from the realm rather than remain among a people who had fallen from the gods' favor.

Such sentiments found willing audiences wherever fear exceeded understanding. There were, however, those of greater learning whose conclusions, though more restrained, differed little in substance. A number of educated men argued that the long Valyrian practice of binding dragons to the service of mankind represented a profound violation of the natural order. According to this view, the southward flight of the dragons was neither illness nor instinct, but a long-delayed correction. The beasts, they claimed, had at last begun to free themselves from centuries of unnatural dependence upon human masters.
